steps.title: Pinpoint the needed evidence
steps.content: The claim needs evidence that Bianchini used a dot in a number where the dot marks a non-whole value. That would show an early decimal point in Bianchini's own calculations, not merely a decorative or separating mark.

steps.title: Reject unrelated findings
steps.content: Choice A weakens the claim because ordinary dots could separate letters or whole numbers. Choice B concerns Clavius, not Bianchini. Choice D says some Bianchini calculations used whole numbers and were inaccurate, which cuts against the decimal-point claim.

steps.title: Use Bianchini's table
steps.content: Choice C supplies the needed evidence: a 1440s Bianchini manuscript has a dot between two numbers in a context suggesting a non-whole value. That directly supports the underlined claim about the earliest decimal point. The answer is C.
